description: Controlled-jitter A/B scenario. Targets a synthetic /test/jitter endpoint that emits one fresh `data-time="<ms>"` attribute per request, with the rest of the page byte-stable. A minimal reference implementation lives in `examples/jitter_endpoint.py` — start it locally before running this scenario. Designed to demonstrate the marginal cache-friendliness gain of act_receipt.v1's dom_region_hash noise-stripping vs a generic jitter-strip.
rationale_target_choice: AB1 (iana.org) baseline shows both arms ≥ 0.80 cache-friendly — a generic strip is already highly effective on stable targets. To demonstrate treatment > control we need a target whose jitter ESCAPES the control's strip but is CAUGHT by the receipt's dom_region_hash noise patterns. The /test/jitter endpoint isolates that variable: only data-time varies, and data-time is in DOM_NOISE_ATTR_PATTERNS, so receipt strips it while control's full_html capture surfaces it.

control.description: Captures raw HTML (full_html=true) — data-time bytes flow into control's hash and aren't in JITTER_FIELDS, so control sees N unique hashes.

treatment.description: Receipt's dom_region_hash(html) strips data-time per DOM_NOISE_ATTR_PATTERNS before SHA-256. canonical_bytes also strips observability + dom_region_size_bytes. Expected stable hash.

expected_outcome.control_cache_friendly_score: ~ 1/N (each run gets unique data-time ms; full_html bytes differ per run; unique_hashes ≈ N)
expected_outcome.treatment_cache_friendly_score: 1.0 (receipt strips data-time; canonical bytes identical across runs)
expected_outcome.delta_interpretation: Strongly positive delta (e.g. +80pp) = act_receipt.v1's noise-pattern strip is providing the cache-friendliness improvement it was designed for. Negative or zero delta = bug in noise pattern handling — investigate.
